name: Hunahpu
description: Hunahpu, one of the Hero Twins in Maya mythology, stands as a pivotal figure within the Popol Vuh, the sacred book of the K'iche' Maya. Born to Hun Hunahpu and Xquic, a maiden impregnated by the spittle of Hun Hunahpu's severed head, Hunahpu embodies themes of rebirth, resilience, and the triumph of good over evil. He and his twin brother, Xbalanque, are renowned for their extraordinary abilities and their eventual defeat of the Lords of Xibalba, the Maya underworld. Hunahpu is often associated with skill, strategy, and the sun, contrasting with his brother's connection to the moon. Their journey through Xibalba represents a cyclical narrative of death and regeneration, central to the Maya worldview. Hunahpu's legacy extends beyond his heroic exploits; he is considered an ancestor figure, symbolizing the potential for humanity to overcome adversity and achieve spiritual enlightenment. The brothers' actions shaped the current era, establishing the world as it is known today.
subtitle: One Hunter

keyMyths.title: The Conception of the Hero Twins
keyMyths.description: Hunahpu's story begins with his father, Hun Hunahpu, being tricked and sacrificed by the Lords of Xibalba. Hun Hunahpu's head is placed in a calabash tree, and when Xquic, the daughter of one of the lords, approaches the tree, the head spits into her hand, impregnating her. She flees Xibalba and is taken in by Hun Hunahpu's mother, Ixmucane. This miraculous conception sets the stage for the birth of Hunahpu and his brother, Xbalanque, and their eventual quest for revenge.
keyMyths.source: Tedlock, Dennis. *Popol Vuh: The Definitive Edition.* Simon & Schuster, 1996.

keyMyths.title: The Ballgame in Xibalba
keyMyths.description: Hunahpu and Xbalanque are summoned to Xibalba to play a ballgame against the Lords of the Underworld. The ballgame is not just a sport but a deadly contest, with the Lords using trickery and traps to try and defeat the twins. Hunahpu and Xbalanque demonstrate their cunning and skill, overcoming many obstacles and challenges set before them, including houses of knives, darkness, and jaguars. Their ability to navigate these dangers highlights their divine nature and strategic brilliance.
keyMyths.source: Christenson, Allen J. *Popol Vuh: The Sacred Book of the Maya.* University of Oklahoma Press, 2007.

keyMyths.title: The Defeat of the Lords of Xibalba
keyMyths.description: After enduring numerous trials, Hunahpu and Xbalanque finally confront the Lords of Xibalba in a final, decisive ballgame. The twins use their intelligence and magical abilities to outwit and ultimately defeat the Lords, avenging the death of their father and uncle. This victory restores balance to the cosmos and marks a turning point in Maya mythology, establishing the triumph of good over evil and paving the way for the creation of humanity. They trick the lords into sacrificing them, then resurrect themselves, demonstrating their power.
keyMyths.source: Recinos, Adrián. *Popol Vuh: The Sacred Book of the Ancient Quiché Maya.* University of Oklahoma Press, 1950.
